channel.title: Andrew D.F. Simon – endangered species
channel.description: Recent content in endangered species on Andrew D.F. Simon

item.title: Tracking down Roell's brotherella moss at the Cheakamus Centre
item.description:
In May 2025, after several trips that had turned up nothing, I finally found Roell’s brotherella moss (Brotherella roellii) growing on an old-growth western redcedar snag at the Cheakamus Centre in Squamish, BC. The site alone was worth the search—like an ancient ruin, cast in rays of light which beamed down through a gap in the canopy.

Brotherella roellii is endangered in Canada and, as far as anyone knows, found nowhere else on Earth. It was once also known from the United States, but is now believed to be extirpated there, leaving a scatter of populations across Howe Sound, the Lower Mainland, and the Fraser Valley as its entire remaining global range.

This particular population was first recorded in 1916 and last seen in 2006. Confirming it was still there—healthy, and possibly one of the most robust populations known in the region—was great news for the Howe Sound Biosphere Region. The species appears to be gone from at least one of its historical sites elsewhere in Squamish, which makes protected refuges like the Cheakamus Centre increasingly important as development continues to fragment lowland coastal rainforests in British Columbia.

Since then, working with Emma Menchions (UBC), we’ve been investigating how light, moisture, and forest structure interact to shape where the moss can persist.

iNaturalist featured the observation as an Observation of the Day, and the find later helped contribute to the Squamish River watershed’s recognition as a Key Biodiversity Area of global significance—owed in part to the region holding roughly 10% of this species’ entire known population.
